meta.description: Likelihood ratio tables for Bayesian posterior update layer. Each question answer carries an LR that updates the ML prior via: posterior_odds = prior_odds × LR(answer).
meta.independence_warning: Naïve Bayes assumed. Do not select two correlated questions for the same condition (e.g. nocturnal choking/gasping + snoring are partially redundant for sleep_disorder; use the highest-LR question only).

confidence_levels.high: LR from meta-analysis or multi-centre validation study
confidence_levels.medium: LR from single well-designed study or derived from published sensitivity/specificity
confidence_levels.low: LR estimated from epidemiological data or expert consensus — treat as placeholder, verify before release

confounders._note: Confounder questions are asked first. A high depression or anxiety score reduces the posterior probability of ALL physical conditions by a global multiplier (see bayesian_updater.py). Screen confounders before condition-specific questions.

questions.text: Over the last two weeks, how often have you had little interest or pleasure in doing things?

answer_options.label: On more than half the days

questions.text: Over the last two weeks, how often have you felt down, depressed or hopeless?

answer_options.label: On more than half the days

thresholds.label: positive
thresholds.lr_note: PHQ-2 ≥3 positive screen. Depression is a strong alternative explanation for fatigue; downgrades physical condition priors.
thresholds.lr_source: Kroenke K et al., Med Care. 2003;41(11):1284-92. Sensitivity 83%, Specificity 90% for MDD → LR+ 8.3, LR- 0.19.

thresholds.label: subthreshold
thresholds.lr_note: Mild depressive symptoms. Modest downgrade to physical condition priors.

thresholds.label: negative
thresholds.lr_note: Depression screened out — slight upward adjustment to physical conditions.
thresholds.lr_source: Kroenke K et al., Med Care. 2003. LR- 0.19 for MDD.

questions.text: Over the last two weeks, how often have you felt nervous, anxious or on edge?

answer_options.label: On more than half the days

questions.text: Over the last two weeks, how often have you not been able to stop or control worrying?

answer_options.label: On more than half the days

thresholds.label: positive
thresholds.lr_note: GAD-2 ≥3 positive screen. Anxiety is an independent cause of fatigue; downgrades physical condition priors.
thresholds.lr_source: Kroenke K et al., Ann Intern Med. 2007;146(5):317-25. Sensitivity 86%, Specificity 83% for GAD → LR+ 5.1, LR- 0.17.

questions.text: Do you have heavy periods lasting more than 7 days, with at least 3 days being heavy or very heavy?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Fraser IS et al., Fertil Steril. 2011. Prevalence of HMB in women — epidemiological framing, LR+ 2.4 not independently confirmed as diagnostic-accuracy value for anemia. Low confidence.

questions.text: Have you noticed any unusual blood loss — for example in your urine, stools, nosebleeds, or elsewhere?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Goddard AF et al., Gut. 2011. Overt GI blood loss sensitivity ~30%, specificity ~91% for significant anemia. Conservative estimate for self-report.

questions.text: Have you donated blood in the last 12 months?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Cable RG et al., Transfusion. 2011. Regular donors have ~3× higher rate of iron depletion leading to anemia.

questions.text: Are you vegetarian or vegan?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Pawlak R et al., Nutr Rev. 2013. Vegetarians have ~2× higher prevalence of iron deficiency anemia. Discounted for anemia vs iron deficiency.

questions.text: For how long have you been experiencing this tiredness every day?

answer_options.label: Less than 4 weeks
answer_options.lr_note: Acute onset less typical for anemia (which is often gradual).

answer_options.label: More than 12 weeks up to 6 months

questions.text: Do you have heavy periods lasting more than 7 days, with at least 3 days being heavy or very heavy?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Munro MG et al., Int J Gynaecol Obstet. 2011. HMB is the leading cause of iron deficiency in premenopausal women. Higher LR here than for anemia as iron deficiency precedes anemia.

questions.text: Are you vegetarian or vegan?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Pawlak R et al., Nutr Rev. 2013. Pooled prevalence of iron deficiency ~40% in vegetarians vs ~16% in omnivores.

questions.text: Have you noticed any unusual blood loss — for example in your urine, stools, nosebleeds, or elsewhere?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Goddard AF et al., Gut. 2011. Higher LR than for anemia — blood loss is the most direct cause of iron deficiency.

questions.text: Have you donated blood in the last 12 months?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Cable RG et al., Transfusion. 2011. Iron depletion in donors well-documented; stronger signal for iron deficiency than for frank anemia.

questions.text: Do you ever have strong cravings to chew ice, raw starch, dirt, or other non-food substances?
questions.supplementary_note: Pica/pagophagia — not in FDDA but highly specific for iron deficiency. Add to follow-up question set.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Kettaneh A et al., Am J Med. 2005. Pagophagia (ice craving) and pica association with iron deficiency — source confirmed in PubMed, but full-text LR+ not independently reproduced. Medium confidence.

questions.text: Have you lost or gained more than 5% of your body weight in the last 3 months?

answer_options.label: Yes — gained weight
answer_options.lr_source: Koulouri O et al. Clin Endocrinol. 2013 (subclinical hypothyroid signs dataset). Weight increase LR+ 1.62 (<60y) and 1.29 (≥60y). Older Zulewski 1997 estimate of 3.0 is not supported by more recent evidence.

answer_options.label: Yes — lost weight
answer_options.lr_note: Weight loss argues against hypothyroidism; may suggest hyperthyroidism or another condition.

questions.text: Do you often feel cold when others around you do not?
questions.supplementary_note: Cold intolerance — not in FDDA but strong hypothyroid indicator from Zulewski scoring.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Zulewski H et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1997. Cold intolerance LR+3.5 cited; exact value not independently confirmed from accessible full text — treat as medium confidence.

questions.text: Have you noticed your skin becoming drier, or your hair becoming coarser or falling out more than usual?
questions.supplementary_note: Dry skin + hair changes composite — not in FDDA, commonly used in thyroid scoring.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Koulouri O et al. Clin Endocrinol. 2013. Coarse skin LR+ 10.0 (<60y) and 5.0 (≥60y). Table uses age-averaged lower bound 5.0 as conservative estimate; higher in younger patients.

questions.text: Have you been constipated more than usual (fewer than 3 bowel movements per week)?
questions.supplementary_note: Constipation — not in FDDA, part of standard hypothyroid symptom screens.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Zulewski H et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 1997. Constipation sensitivity 48%, specificity 76%.

questions.text: For how long have you been experiencing this tiredness every day?

answer_options.label: Less than 4 weeks
answer_options.lr_note: Hypothyroidism typically causes gradual onset fatigue. Acute onset argues against it.

answer_options.label: More than 12 weeks up to 6 months

questions.text: Have you noticed blood in your urine, or has your urine appeared dark, foamy, or brown?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Grossfeld GD et al., Am Fam Physician. 2001. Policy paper on asymptomatic microscopic hematuria — not a formal diagnostic-accuracy study. LR+ is a clinical estimate; treat as low confidence.

questions.text: Have you noticed any swelling in your ankles, feet, or around your eyes, particularly in the morning?
questions.supplementary_note: Edema — classic CKD symptom not covered by FDDA.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: O'Callaghan CA, The Renal System at a Glance. Clinical estimate only. No primary diagnostic-accuracy study for edema → CKD LR confirmed from accessible evidence.

questions.text: Do you wake up at night to urinate, and has this become more frequent recently?
questions.supplementary_note: Nocturia — important early CKD symptom, also used in sleep disorder screening but different mechanism.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical estimate. Nocturia is non-specific but relevant in context of elevated kidney ML prior.

questions.text: Have you lost or gained more than 5% of your body weight in the last 3 months without trying?

answer_options.label: Yes — lost weight unintentionally
answer_options.lr_note: Unintentional weight loss is a flag for advanced kidney disease. LR estimate only.

questions.text: Do you wake up at night choking, gasping for air, or feeling like you cannot breathe?
questions.supplementary_note: Rephrased from 'witnessed apnea' to nocturnal choking/gasping — the JAMA RCE identifies this as the most useful self-reported symptom for OSA. Witnessed apnea requires a bed partner; this question captures the patient-reported equivalent.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Joosten SA et al., JAMA. 2016 (Rational Clinical Examination). Nocturnal choking or gasping: summary LR+ 3.3 (95% CI 2.1–4.6) at AHI ≥10/h threshold. Most useful single symptom for OSA diagnosis.

questions.text: Do you snore loudly (loud enough to be heard through a closed door)?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Joosten SA et al., JAMA. 2016 (Rational Clinical Examination). Snoring summary LR+ 1.1 (95% CI 1.0–1.1). Snoring is common in OSA but not diagnostically useful alone. Prior estimate of 1.8 was overstated.

questions.text: Do you fall asleep involuntarily during the day — for example while driving, watching TV, or in a meeting?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Johns MW, Sleep. 1991. Epworth Sleepiness Scale background. Involuntary daytime sleep is a reliable marker of pathological hypersomnolence.

questions.text: Even when you sleep for a full night, do you wake up feeling unrefreshed or as tired as when you went to bed?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical estimate. Non-restorative sleep is a defining feature of sleep disorders including OSA and insomnia disorder.

questions.text: Do you have difficulty falling or staying asleep at least 3 nights per week?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Morin CM et al., Lancet. 2012. Insomnia diagnosis criterion: difficulty 3+ nights/week. Sensitivity ~70%, Specificity ~65% for insomnia disorder.

liver._scope_note: The ML liver model detects general hepatic dysfunction (elevated ALAT pattern). Questions below include both general liver disease markers and cirrhosis-specific signs (spider nevi, ascites) from JAMA RCE meta-analysis. LRs for spider nevi and ascites are validated for cirrhosis specifically — they are most relevant when the ML prior is high (≥0.50).

questions.text: How many standard alcoholic drinks do you have per week? (1 drink = 1 glass of wine, 1 bottle of beer, or 1 shot of spirits)

answer_options.label: More than 14 per week (men) or more than 7 per week (women)
answer_options.lr_source: O'Shea RS et al., Hepatology. 2010. AASLD guideline. Heavy alcohol use is primary risk for alcoholic liver disease; LR+ estimate, not from formal diagnostic-accuracy study.

answer_options.label: Fewer than 7 (men) or fewer than 3 (women) per week
I don't drink
answer_options.lr_note: Absence of alcohol strongly argues against alcoholic liver disease; shifts differential toward viral or metabolic causes.

questions.text: Have you noticed small red spider-like marks on your skin, especially on your chest, shoulders, or face?
questions.supplementary_note: Spider nevi — physical sign of cirrhosis. Self-reported but identifiable by patients; ask only when ML prior ≥0.50.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Udell JA et al., JAMA. 2012 (Rational Clinical Examination: Does This Patient Have Cirrhosis?). Spider nevi LR+ 4.3 (95% CI 2.4–6.2) in meta-analysis of 86 studies, 19,533 patients.

answer_options.label: No
answer_options.lr_source: Udell JA et al., JAMA. 2012.

questions.text: Has your abdomen become noticeably swollen or distended, or have you been told you have fluid in your belly?
questions.supplementary_note: Ascites — strongest self-reportable cirrhosis sign from JAMA RCE. Ask only when ML prior ≥0.50.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Udell JA et al., JAMA. 2012 (Rational Clinical Examination: Does This Patient Have Cirrhosis?). Ascites LR+ 7.2 (95% CI 2.9–12) — most informative single physical finding for cirrhosis.

answer_options.label: No
answer_options.lr_source: Udell JA et al., JAMA. 2012. Absence of hepatomegaly LR- 0.37 (95% CI 0.24–0.51); ascites absence is similar.

questions.text: Have you noticed any yellowing of your skin or the whites of your eyes?
questions.supplementary_note: Jaundice — clinically relevant for both hepatitis and cirrhosis.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Udell JA et al., JAMA. 2012 (Rational Clinical Examination: Does This Patient Have Cirrhosis?). Jaundice LR+ 3.8 (95% CI 2.0–7.2).

answer_options.label: No
answer_options.lr_note: Many liver disease cases are anicteric — absence of jaundice does not rule out liver pathology.
answer_options.lr_source: Udell JAMA 2012.

questions.text: Have you lost weight unintentionally — more than 5% of your body weight in the last 3 months?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical estimate. Unintentional weight loss is a feature of decompensated liver disease.

questions.text: Have you gained more than 5% of your body weight in the last 3 months?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical estimate. Recent weight gain is a known risk factor for insulin resistance. LR modest and non-specific.

questions.text: Do you feel unusually thirsty most days, even when you have had enough to drink?
questions.supplementary_note: Polydipsia — classic hyperglycaemia symptom. Not in FDDA.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Manley SE et al., Diabet Med. 2000. Polydipsia sensitivity cited but full-text LR not independently reproduced. Clinical estimate; treat as low confidence.

questions.text: Do you urinate more frequently than usual, including waking at night to urinate?
questions.supplementary_note: Polyuria — not in FDDA.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Gregg EW et al., Ann Intern Med. 2001. Polyuria sensitivity ~48%, specificity ~80% for undiagnosed diabetes.

questions.text: Overall, how would you describe your level of physical activity?

answer_options.label: I don't exercise at all
answer_options.lr_note: Sedentary lifestyle is the primary modifiable risk factor for prediabetes.

answer_options.label: Intensively (more than 1 hour of vigorous sport/day)

questions.text: Have you lost more than 5% of your body weight in the last 3 months without trying?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Unexplained weight loss as marker of systemic inflammation — clinical consensus, no primary diagnostic-accuracy study LR confirmed from accessible evidence.

questions.text: Have you had a fever, persistent sore throat, or an infection in the last 6 weeks?
questions.supplementary_note: Recent infection — important acute inflammation trigger. Not in FDDA.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_note: Post-infectious fatigue and active infection are the most common causes of elevated CRP. Clinical estimate.
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical estimate. No primary diagnostic-accuracy LR source identified for 'recent infection/fever → CRP elevation' as symptom question.

questions.text: Do you have pain, swelling, or stiffness in your joints, especially in the morning?
questions.supplementary_note: Joint symptoms — key differentiator for autoimmune/rheumatic inflammation.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Aletaha D et al., Arthritis Rheum. 2010. Morning stiffness + joint swelling is a core ACR criterion for rheumatoid arthritis and other systemic inflammatory conditions.

questions.text: For how long have you been experiencing this tiredness every day?

answer_options.label: Less than 4 weeks
answer_options.lr_note: Acute-onset fatigue is more consistent with active infection or acute inflammatory episode.

answer_options.label: More than 12 weeks up to 6 months
More than 6 months
answer_options.lr_note: Chronic fatigue >6 months is less typical for simple acute inflammation.

questions.text: How many standard alcoholic drinks do you have per week?

answer_options.label: More than 14 per week (men) or more than 7 per week (women)
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical guideline estimate. Heavy alcohol use causes hypomagnesaemia, hypokalaemia. Fallon MB et al., Hepatology 1992.

answer_options.label: Fewer than 3 per week or none

questions.text: Have you had prolonged vomiting or diarrhoea (more than 2 days) in the past 2 weeks?
questions.supplementary_note: GI fluid loss — primary cause of acute electrolyte disturbance. Not in FDDA.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_note: Acute GI losses are the leading cause of clinically significant hyponatraemia/hypokalaemia. Clinical estimate.
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical estimate. GI fluid loss as electrolyte risk — physiologically supported but LR+ 3.5 not sourced from a formal diagnostic-accuracy study in accessible evidence.

questions.text: Do you regularly experience muscle cramps, muscle weakness, or muscle twitching?
questions.supplementary_note: Muscle symptoms of electrolyte imbalance — not in FDDA.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Gennari FJ, N Engl J Med. 1998. Muscle cramps/weakness is a clinical marker of hypokalaemia and hypomagnesaemia. LR is modest given non-specificity.

questions.text: Do you take any medications regularly? If yes, do they include diuretics (water pills), laxatives, or antacids?

answer_options.label: Yes — taking diuretics, laxatives, or antacids
answer_options.lr_source: Rastegar A et al., NEJM. 2001. Diuretics are the most common cause of electrolyte imbalance — this is a review, not a diagnostic-accuracy study. LR+ is a clinical estimate.

questions.text: How many standard alcoholic drinks do you have per week?

answer_options.label: More than 14 per week (men) or more than 7 per week (women)
answer_options.lr_source: O'Shea RS et al., Hepatology. 2010. Heavy alcohol is the primary cause of alcoholic hepatitis.

answer_options.label: Fewer than 3 per week or none
answer_options.lr_note: Non-drinkers are much more likely to have viral hepatitis as the cause if the ML prior is elevated.

questions.text: Have you noticed any yellowing of your skin or the whites of your eyes?
questions.supplementary_note: Jaundice — highly specific for hepatic disease.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Pratt DS et al., cited in Rational Clinical Examination series. Jaundice LR+ 8.0 not independently confirmed from accessible primary text; may conflate hepatitis and other causes. Low confidence.

answer_options.label: No
answer_options.lr_note: Absence of jaundice does not rule out hepatitis — many cases are anicteric.

questions.text: Have you had any of the following in the past: a blood transfusion before 1992, tattooing or piercing with shared equipment, or intravenous drug use?
questions.supplementary_note: Hepatitis C risk factors — not in FDDA but critical for viral hepatitis differential.

answer_options.label: Yes to any of the above
answer_options.lr_source: Alter MJ, NEJM. 1999. HCV risk factors paper — describes exposure prevalence, not a formal symptom LR diagnostic-accuracy study. LR+ 4.5 is a clinical estimate.

questions.text: Have you lost weight unintentionally in the last 3 months?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_note: Unintentional weight loss is a feature of advanced hepatic disease or active viral hepatitis.

questions.text: Do you think you might be going through the menopause transition or perimenopause?
questions.supplementary_note: Self-assessment of perimenopausal status — best single negative predictor per JAMA RCE meta-analysis.

answer_options.label: Yes, I think I might be
answer_options.lr_source: Politi MC et al., JAMA. 2008 (Rational Clinical Examination). Self-assessment LR+ range 1.53–2.13 across studies; midpoint used.

answer_options.label: No, I don't think so
answer_options.lr_source: Politi MC et al., JAMA. 2008. Self-assessment LR- range 0.18–0.36; most informative negative predictor in the review.

questions.text: Do you experience hot flushes — sudden feelings of intense heat in your face, neck, or chest?
questions.supplementary_note: Hot flushes — defining vasomotor symptom. LR corrected downward from initial estimate using JAMA meta-analysis.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Politi MC et al., JAMA. 2008 (Rational Clinical Examination). Hot flash LR+ range 2.15–4.06 across studies; midpoint 3.1 used. Prior estimate of 7.0 was unsupported.

questions.text: Do you wake up at night drenched in sweat, even when your bedroom is not hot?
questions.supplementary_note: Night sweats — LR corrected from 4.5 to 1.90 using JAMA meta-analysis data.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Politi MC et al., JAMA. 2008 (Rational Clinical Examination). Night sweats LR+ 1.90 (95% CI 1.63–2.21). Prior estimate of 4.5 was not meta-analysis-derived.

questions.text: Have you noticed vaginal dryness or discomfort during sex that is new or getting worse?
questions.supplementary_note: Vaginal dryness — genitourinary symptom of menopause (GSM). From JAMA RCE.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Politi MC et al., JAMA. 2008 (Rational Clinical Examination). Vaginal dryness LR+ range 1.48–3.79; midpoint 2.6 used.

questions.text: Have your menstrual cycles become irregular — either shorter, longer, or skipping months?
questions.supplementary_note: Cycle irregularity — not in FDDA.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Harlow SD et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2012. STRAW+10 staging criteria. Cycle irregularity ≥7 days: strong marker of early menopausal transition.

questions.text: Do you have heavy periods lasting more than 7 days, with at least 3 days being heavy or very heavy?

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Harlow SD et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2012. Heavy or irregular bleeding is common in late perimenopause due to anovulatory cycles.

questions.text: Have you noticed changes in your mood, such as increased irritability, anxiety, or low mood, particularly linked to your cycle?
questions.supplementary_note: Mood/cognitive changes — common perimenopause symptom but overlaps with depression confounder. Weight carefully.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Freeman EW et al., Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2006. Mood symptoms sensitivity ~55%, specificity ~75% in perimenopause vs premenopause.

questions.text: Do you take metformin most days, or have you taken it regularly for at least 6 months?
questions.supplementary_note: Long-term metformin use is a recognized B12 deficiency risk factor.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: NICE vitamin B12 deficiency evidence review (2024) and contemporary consensus reviews list metformin as a common risk factor for low B12.

questions.text: Do you take acid-suppressing medication like omeprazole, pantoprazole, or famotidine most days for more than 3 months?
questions.supplementary_note: Chronic acid suppression can impair dietary B12 absorption.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Risk-factor estimate based on guideline and review evidence linking chronic PPI or H2 blocker use with lower B12 status.

questions.text: Do you avoid most animal products, or follow a vegan diet?
questions.supplementary_note: Low animal-product intake lowers B12 intake unless supplementation is consistent.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Dietary-risk estimate supported by clinical reviews and guidelines on B12 deficiency in low-animal-product diets.

questions.text: Have you ever been told you have pernicious anemia, autoimmune gastritis, Crohn's disease, celiac disease, or had bowel surgery affecting absorption?
questions.supplementary_note: Autoimmune or malabsorptive causes are among the strongest clinical risk factors for true B12 deficiency.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: NICE vitamin B12 deficiency evidence review (2024). Pernicious anemia and malabsorption syndromes are major etiologic risk factors.

questions.text: Have you had new tingling or numbness in your hands or feet, trouble with balance, or unexplained memory changes?
questions.supplementary_note: Neurologic symptoms are clinically important for B12 deficiency but are not highly specific.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical heuristic based on B12 deficiency neurology reviews; symptom LR is kept modest because formal diagnostic-accuracy evidence is limited.

questions.text: Do you spend very little time in direct sunlight most weeks, or are you mostly indoors or fully covered when outside?
questions.supplementary_note: Low UV exposure is a major driver of low vitamin D status.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements vitamin D professional fact sheet. Limited sunlight exposure is a recognized risk factor for low 25(OH)D.

questions.text: Have you been told you have obesity, or is your BMI 30 or above?
questions.supplementary_note: Obesity is consistently associated with lower measured 25(OH)D levels.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Risk-factor estimate based on review evidence and NIH guidance identifying obesity as a common vitamin D deficiency risk factor.

questions.text: Have you ever been told you have celiac disease, Crohn's disease, pancreatic disease, or had bowel surgery that affects absorption?
questions.supplementary_note: Fat malabsorption is a common clinical cause of vitamin D deficiency.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Vitamin D deficiency and osteomalacia reviews consistently identify malabsorption syndromes as major causes of deficiency.

questions.text: Have you been told you have chronic kidney disease or chronic liver disease?
questions.supplementary_note: Kidney and liver disease can reduce vitamin D activation or handling.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Clinical review evidence identifies CKD and chronic liver disease as important risk factors for low active vitamin D states.

questions.text: Do you have diffuse bone pain, marked muscle weakness when climbing stairs or rising from a chair, or frequent falls?
questions.supplementary_note: These symptoms fit osteomalacia-type presentations but are nonspecific.

answer_options.label: Yes
answer_options.lr_source: Osteomalacia and vitamin D deficiency reviews describe proximal muscle weakness and bone pain as common manifestations; LR kept low because specificity is limited.
